title: Lesson 222
transcript: Let's talk about business continuity management. This is where we're going to talk about terms like BCP and DRP and BCM. And we're going to later in the next module, we're going to get into a whole bunch of other terms, three letter acronyms like RTO and RPO and et cetera. So it gets kind of complicated quickly, but let's start with something simple. Let's start with what is a disaster? Here's a definition of a disaster that I like. It's a sudden, unplanned, calamitous event that causes great damage or destruction. That's a disaster. And business continuity management is ultimately about identifying when these disasters might occur. What ones do you need to be worried about? Floods, hurricanes, earthquakes, fires, things like that. And then figuring out how do you actually deal with these major disasters. And so ultimately, the focus of business continuity management is about figuring out what disasters might befall an organization and how best to deal with them. But importantly, in business continuity management, we don't focus on every single business process or system within an organization. We only focus on the most critical and essential business functions. And so a big part of business continuity management is actually figuring out what is most critical and essential to the business. What is of greatest focus? And it's for these most critical systems processes that we create DRP plans and BCP plans. So let's talk through these. What are these things? Let's first talk about disaster recovery plans, DRPs. Disaster recovery plans focus on recovering specific systems or technologies. So let's take the payroll system as an example. The disaster recovery plan would focus on recovering the payroll system if that system blew up. And let's say it took six weeks for the disaster recovery plan to recover the payroll system. The DRP plan would be entirely focused on recovering that specific system. BCP plans, on the other hand, business continuity plans, focus on overall business processes. So let's continue with the payroll example. The BCP plan would focus on the overall process of making sure our employees are paid. And in the example I just gave about a DRP plan, I said it might take six weeks for the DRP plan to recover the payroll system. I don't know about you, but I'd be a lot less excited to show up for work if I wasn't being paid for six weeks. So while the DRP plan was focused on recovering the system, the BCP plan would contemplate how do we make sure we actually keep paying our employees to make sure those employees actually show up for work. And so it might be a really slow, really manual, really painful process, but the BCP plan might contemplate having a stack of blank checks, and someone would have to figure out how to still use a pen again, fill in name, fill in the amounts, and then use the system that I'm not even sure if it exists anymore, but I think it's called the mail system, and manually mail these things out. And so that's the key difference between BCP and DRP. DRP focuses on specific systems technologies. BCP focuses on the overall business processes. Now the final bit of terminology we'll talk about here is business continuity management, BCM. Business continuity management is the business process of making sure that all these BCP plans are created, all these DRP plans are created, that they're updated, maintained, tested, that there are people who, This is their responsibility. They have accountability for all of this, that this function is paid for, that we create, that we go through the BIA process that we'll talk about in the next module. So business continuity management is the overall business process of making sure all of this disaster recovery stuff is done within a business.
